Reference Object  ·  Catalogue No. RO-BRONZE-036  ·  Bronze / Archaeometallurgy

Bronze Ritual Tripod Vessel (Ding-type)

Lidded ding vessel with two upright loop handles, taotie and interlace ornament bands, and paired animal-form lid knobs

Attribution: Late Shang – Western Zhou, ca. 11th–8th century BCE
Material: archaic Cu–Pb–Sn–Sb(–As) leaded bronze
Scientific Director: Nona D. Dronova, D.Sc. (Engineering), Ph.D. (Geology and Mineralogy)
Fig. 1 — Full view: lidded tripod vessel with upright loop handles, paired animal-form lid knobs, taotie and interlace ornament bands.
Fig. 2 — Body/leg junction, detail: ornament band and foot form.
Fig. 3 — Leg and body ornament, detail: interlace/meander ground pattern with roundel.
Fig. 4 — Lid interior, open, showing casting surface and mineral encrustation.
Fig. 5 — Lid, overhead view: central knob, taotie band, paired lid figures.

01 Object Description and Attribution

The vessel is a bronze ritual tripod of ding-type form: a rounded body on three legs, fitted with a domed lid carrying a central disc-form knob and two small animal-form figures set at the lid rim (Fig. 5). Two upright loop handles rise from the body rim (Fig. 1). The body carries two registers of taotie (饕餮) and interlace ornament divided by a raised band (Figs. 1–2), with a meander/interlace ground pattern and raised roundels on the legs (Fig. 3). No inscription is visible. Surface condition shows a well-developed, multi-toned archaeological patina — green copper-corrosion zones, buff soil encrustation, and localised mineral deposits on the lid interior (Fig. 4) — consistent with prolonged natural burial.

The vessel is attributed to the late Shang – Western Zhou period, ca. 11th–8th century BCE, on the combined basis of alloy recipe, casting technology, and corrosion stratigraphy set out below, which correspond closely to published and museum comparanda from Anyang and Zhouyuan.

02 SEM–EDX Diagnostic Analysis

Two independent SEM–EDX sessions were carried out by the author, together covering ten micro-zones across two patina survey areas, plus a separate two-point matrix analysis of a hand-removed edge shaving and a three-point analysis of mineral powder recovered from internal recesses.

2.1 Patina, first surface area (5-point survey)

ElementRange (at.%)Note
Oup to 64.2dense oxide layer, long-term exposure
Sn6.6–9.9archaic alloying component
Sbup to 3.7archaic alloy marker, rare in modern forgeries
Pb1.0–5.0leaded-bronze recipe
Cu4.4–30.2residual metal / corroded inclusion
Cl, Na, P, Cpresent, variablesoil/organic contact

2.2 Patina, second surface area

ElementRange (at.%)Note
Pbup to 23.6 (spot 1)deep oxidation of leaded component (PbO/PbCl₂)
Cu21–26.7malachite / cuprite / tenorite
Oup to 58.5principal corrosion oxide phase
Sup to 6.6 (spot 3)local Cu₂S formation
Clup to 10saline-soil chloride patina, no acid-etch signature
Naup to 13.9soil-derived, within natural range
Cup to 28.3organic residue / carbonate
Pup to 0.8biological / apatite-type contamination

Combining both sessions across all ten patina micro-zones, oxygen is distributed uniformly through the crust — confirming deep mineralisation — while carbon shows localised concentration consistent with organic interaction or carbonatisation in discrete zones. SEM imaging shows platy layering, exfoliation, and dendritic/pseudomorphic aggregates: a microstructure typical of slow, multi-stage natural corrosion developing over centuries, not of rapid artificial ageing. No excess chlorine suggestive of acid etching and no thermal-shock textures were observed at any of the ten zones.

2.3 Metallic matrix — edge shaving (2-point survey)

ElementPoint 1 — Pb-rich inclusion (at.%)Point 3 — matrix (at.%)
Cu52.747.3
Pb23.03.8
C21.736.0
Sn1.51.2
Sb1.11.1
O—8.2
F—1.5
As—0.9

Point 1’s 23.0% lead reading is a discrete, undissolved lead inclusion — lead did not fully dissolve into the copper–tin matrix during casting and segregated as a separate phase on cooling, a recognised feature of late Shang to Western Zhou leaded-bronze practice. Point 3, the surrounding matrix, carries lead at a lower 3.8% together with the Sb–Sn–As natural-ore impurity suite, indicating unrefined, complex sulphide-bearing ore rather than a deliberately purified charge. The comparatively low tin content at both points indicates an alloy optimised for casting fluidity (mould-filling) rather than hardness, consistent with thin-walled ritual-vessel production.

2.4 Powder from internal recesses (3-point survey)

ElementConcentration (at.%)
O37.8
C28.4
Si15.0
Cu8.9
Al7.6
Fe2.4

A silica-rich aluminosilicate assemblage with copper and iron, consistent with soil-derived clay minerals (kaolinite/illite-type) intermixed with secondary copper corrosion products — confirming prolonged burial in a clay-loess type soil matrix, matching the mineral deposits visible on the lid interior (Fig. 4).

Alloy

Cu–Pb–Sn–Sb(–As) leaded bronze, heterogeneous Pb distribution — discrete inclusions and dissolved matrix Pb both present.

Patina

Layered, dendritic, multi-mineral corrosion across ten independently sampled zones — no artificial-ageing signature.

Internal residue

Clay-type aluminosilicate + copper corrosion, matching visible lid-interior deposits.

Casting

Low-tin, high-fluidity alloy recipe consistent with thin-walled ritual-vessel casting technology.

03 Comparative Analysis

ComparandumPeriodPb (%)Note
Ding vessel, Anyang (Henan Museum)Late Shang18–29localised Pb inclusions, comparable heterogeneity
Hu vessel, Sackler Gallery (F1944.12)Western Zhou, 11th–9th c. BCE3–12comparable Cu–Pb–Sn–Sb composition
Bronzes, SanxingduiBronze Ageup to 25lead from Sichuan regional ore
Tripod cauldron, ZhouyuanWestern Zhou, 11th–9th c. BCE2–10comparable Cu–Pb–Sb alloy

The antimony and lead concentrations recorded here correspond closely to published data for late Shang and Western Zhou bronzes, including comparanda from the Palace Museum (Gugong) and Penn Museum collections. Barnard and Smith similarly document antimony and lead concentrations of this order in late Shang and Western Zhou bronzes, supporting the technological attribution proposed here.

04 Conclusion

The bronze tripod vessel displays an archaeologically consistent, naturally formed patina developed through long-term corrosion of an archaic, complex-alloyed leaded bronze (Cu–Pb–Sn–Sb–As). No evidence of artificial ageing, acid treatment, or modern chemical imitation was observed at any of the ten patina micro-zones or in the metallic matrix analyses. The alloy recipe, the heterogeneous distribution of lead, and the layered, multi-mineral corrosion stratigraphy are jointly consistent with mould-cast ritual bronze production of the late Shang to Western Zhou period, ca. 11th–8th century BCE, and correspond closely to published and museum comparanda from Anyang and Zhouyuan.
